return.label: Return
return.detail: Thu 20 Apr · US-Bangla Airlines · ~5:00 PM depart

acts.tx: Depart Dhaka DAC — US-Bangla Airlines to Kolkata CCU. ~1 hr flight.
Arrive Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport (CCU). Clear immigration + bags.
Uber/Ola: CCU → FabHotel Mayfair Tower. ~45 min, INR 600–800.
Check in: FabHotel Mayfair Tower, Kolkata.
Lunch near hotel — Bengali thali or kati roll from a local restaurant. INR 150–250/person.

Jorasanko Thakurbari — the ancestral home of Rabindranath Tagore, now a museum. INR 10/person. Beautiful terracotta architecture; manuscript and painting collections. ~1 hr. Take metro to Girish Park.
Marble Palace (optional) — 19th-century mansion with marble floors, European art, and a private zoo. Free but advance permission required through WBTDC. A 5-min walk from Jorasanko.
Uber to New Town/Rajarhat. ~30 min, INR 200–300.
Biswa Bangla Gate — the iconic golden V-shaped gateway, symbol of a resurgent West Bengal. Free. Illuminated after dark. Great photo spot on the New Town action area road.
Eco Park, Rajarhat — 480-acre urban park with themed gardens, a lake, boating, cycling, and replicas of the Seven Wonders. INR 30/person entry. Boat ride INR 50/person extra.
Uber to Park Street. ~30 min, INR 200–300.
The Lords and Barons, Park Street — one of Kolkata's most celebrated pubs. Live music, craft beer, classic cocktails. Park Street is the soul of KOL nightlife.
Breakfast at a Park Street bakery or hotel — pav bhaji or toast + chai. INR 100–150/person.
Indian Museum, Park Street — oldest and largest museum in India (est. 1814). Fossils, Egyptian mummies, Mughal miniatures, and the famous Bharhut Gallery. INR 20/person. ~1.5 hrs.
Victoria Memorial — stunning white marble monument built for Queen Victoria. Museum inside with colonial-era paintings and artefacts. INR 200/person (foreign visitor). Gardens free. ~1 hr.
Esplanade — lunch at a local dhaba or Peter Cat (famous for chelo kebab, INR 400–600/person). Browse the busy pavement stalls and New Market entrance street.
St Paul's Cathedral — 1847 Gothic Revival cathedral, the oldest of its kind in the subcontinent. Free. Stained-glass windows, Florentine frescoes, peaceful interior.
Princep Ghat — scenic riverside promenade on the Hooghly with Victorian arches and boat views. Free. One of Kolkata's most romantic sunset spots. ~1 hr.
Birla Mandir (Lakshmi Narayan Temple), Ballygunge — ornate white marble temple built in 1970. Free. Open until 9 PM. Serene atmosphere after a busy day.
Dinner at Ballygunge — Mitra Café (iconic since 1963: kosha mangsho, mustard fish), or Oh! Calcutta for refined Bengali cuisine. INR 500–800/person.
Breakfast — mishti doi and luchi from a neighbourhood sweet shop. INR 80–120/person.

BBD Bagh (Dalhousie Square) — Kolkata's colonial core. Writer's Building (1777), GPO, St John's Church, Raj Bhavan. Free walking exploration. ~1 hr. The most concentrated cluster of British-era architecture in India.
New Market (Sir Stuart Hogg Market, 1874) — sprawling indoor-outdoor market: textiles, leather, spices, sweets, street food. Free entry. Great for gifts and local snacks.
Lunch near New Market — roll from Nizam's or Shiraz Hotel (legendary biryani). INR 200–400/person.
Return to FabHotel Mayfair Tower. Rest and freshen up before the temple run.
Uber/Ola to Dakshineshwar. ~30–40 min, INR 250–350.

Dakshineshwar Kali Temple — famous riverside temple where Sri Ramakrishna served as priest. Free. The nahabatkhana, 12 Shiva temples, and the main Kali shrine. Deeply spiritual atmosphere on the Hooghly. Remove footwear.
Ferry launch: Dakshineshwar → Belur Math. INR 5–10/person. ~15 min across the Hooghly — a memorable river crossing at dusk.
Belur Math — the headquarters of the Ramakrishna Mission, blending Hindu, Christian, and Islamic architectural motifs. Free. Grounds open till 7:30 PM. Serene riverside setting.
Dinner back near hotel — Bengali fish curry or chowmein from a Kolkata-Chinese eatery on the way. INR 200–350/person.
INOX multiplex — latest Hindi or English film. INR 250–350/person. Book tickets online beforehand to secure seats.
Last breakfast in Kolkata — rosogolla and toast at a sweet shop. INR 80–120/person.
Final stroll and last-minute shopping on Park Street or New Market. Pick up mishti doi, sandesh, and mustard oil to bring back.
Final lunch — Arsalan or Shiraz Hotel biryani. INR 250–400/person.
Check out of FabHotel Mayfair Tower. Uber to CCU airport. ~45 min, INR 600–800.
Arrive CCU (Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose International Airport). Check-in + immigration + security. US-Bangla Airlines return flight.
Flight departs CCU — US-Bangla Airlines to Dhaka DAC. ~1 hr.
Land Dhaka DAC — Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport, Terminal 1.

budget.note: DAC↔CCU US-Bangla Airlines return, for 2 passengers
Airport transfers × 2 (1,400) · Uber/Ola city rides · metro · ferry launch
Victoria Memorial 400 · Indian Museum 40 · Eco Park 60 · Jorasanko 20 · INOX 700 · misc temples free
Street food + local restaurants ~INR 600/day for two. Biryani, kati rolls, mishti, Bengali thali.
New Market, sweets & snacks to bring home, misc

notes.title: Currency
notes.text: 1 USD ≈ INR 82 · 1 BDT ≈ INR 0.73 (approx, Apr 2023). Carry INR cash — most street vendors, autos, and small eateries don't accept cards.

notes.title: Transport
notes.text: Use Uber or Ola for comfortable point-to-point travel (INR 200–800 per trip). Kolkata Metro is cheap (INR 5–25) and fast for central corridors. Yellow Ambassador taxis are iconic but negotiate the fare upfront.

notes.title: Marble Palace
notes.text: Prior written permission needed from the Mullick family via WBTDC (West Bengal Tourism). Apply at least a day ahead. Visit is free once permitted.

notes.title: Dress Code
notes.text: Remove footwear at all temples — Dakshineshwar, Belur Math, Birla Mandir, Jorasanko. Carry a small bag for shoes. Light, breathable clothing recommended for April heat (32–38°C).

notes.title: Food
notes.text: Must-try: kosha mangsho (dry mutton curry), shorshe ilish (hilsa in mustard sauce), chelo kebab at Peter Cat, Park Street. For sweets: rosogolla, sandesh, mishti doi from KC Das or Balaram Mullick.
